---

## Figure Type

This is a **borehole/well log (каротажная диаграмма)** — a composite geophysical well log from a Soviet-era drilling survey. It appears to be a multi-column strip log printed on long-format paper (consistent with standard Soviet бланк каротажа format), likely representing one or more geophysical logging curves plotted against depth. The log is printed in black ink on light paper and has been folded multiple times, creating visible horizontal fold lines across the image.

---

## Coverage and Subject

The log covers a **single borehole** (скважина), the specific number of which is **[illegible]** due to scan quality and fading. The document appears to originate from the regional survey described in Report 36572, covering map sheets **M-42 and/or N-42** in northern Kazakhstan. The depth range appears to extend from near-surface down to what may be several hundred metres, based on the length of the log strip, but precise depth values on the scale are **[illegible]**. A small legend/header box is visible in the **upper left corner** of the log, which would normally contain the borehole identifier, survey organisation, date, and scale information, but this is too faint to read reliably.

---

## Key Content

The log contains **multiple parallel vertical columns** (tracks), which is characteristic of Soviet composite well logs. The following structural features can be described:

- **Left-side columns**: Appear to contain **lithological/stratigraphic description columns** — narrow ruled columns that would typically encode rock type, core recovery, and formation boundaries. These are faint but show a consistent ruled grid structure throughout.

- **Central track(s)**: Show a **geophysical curve** with moderate to low amplitude variation in the upper portion of the log, transitioning to **higher amplitude, more irregular deflections** in the lower-middle and lower portions. This pattern is consistent with a **natural gamma (ГК, gamma-ray)** or **resistivity (КС, кажущееся сопротивление)** curve.

- **Right-side track(s)**: Show a **second geophysical curve** with notably **high-amplitude, spiky deflections** — particularly prominent in what appears to be the upper third and middle sections of the log. This spiked, high-amplitude signature is characteristic of either a **self-potential (ПС, потенциал самополяризации)** curve or a **high-resolution resistivity log** responding to lithological contrasts.

- **Anomalous zones**: Several depth intervals show pronounced **peaks/spikes** on the right-side curve, suggesting mineralised intervals, hard rock horizons, or significant lithological contrasts. These are most pronounced in approximately the upper 30–40% of the total log length.

- **Lower portion of log**: The curves become more subdued and relatively flat, potentially indicating more homogeneous lithology (possibly basement or monotonous sedimentary sequence).

- A **diagonal line** is visible in the very bottom-right corner, which may represent a **calibration mark**, **depth correction**, or **end-of-log notation** — a common feature on Soviet logs.

- The overall **grey-brown tonal variation** across the scan background reflects **paper degradation and uneven scanning** rather than geological content.

---

- **Depth scale** (left margin): Tick marks and numbers are present at regular intervals but individual values are **[illegible]**. The spacing suggests depth increments of possibly **10 m** per division.

- **Fold-line annotations**: No readable text at fold intersections.

## Research Value

This borehole log provides a continuous **geophysical and lithological record** of subsurface conditions at a specific drill site within the M-42/N-42 survey area, allowing correlation of rock types, identification of mineralised or anomalous intervals, and calibration of surface geophysical data (seismic, gravity, magnetic) against actual subsurface conditions. The high-amplitude anomalies visible on one of the curves could indicate **ore-bearing horizons, dense basement rocks, or fluid-saturated zones** relevant to the survey's mineral potential assessment. For an AI research assistant, this figure — once the borehole number and depth scale are confirmed from the original document — would serve as a critical ground-truth dataset for interpreting the broader geophysical survey results reported in Report 36572.
